title: Share of workers in informal employment vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Share of employed people working in jobs that lack basic social or legal protection and employment benefits. Does not include illegal and illicit activities. Figures refer to [national estimates](#dod:ilo-national-estimates) and may not be fully comparable across countries. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Share of workers in informal employment vs. GDP per capita

Share of employed people working in jobs that lack basic social or legal protection and employment benefits. Does not include illegal and illicit activities. Figures refer to national estimates and may not be fully comparable across countries. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot displays the relationship between the share of workers in informal employment and GDP per capita for various countries in 2024. The vertical axis represents the percentage of informal employment, ranging from 0% to 100%, while the horizontal axis shows GDP per capita in international dollars on a logarithmic scale, spanning from roughly $1,000 to over $100,000. Circle sizes correspond to population, with India appearing as the largest circle. A general downward trend is visible: countries with lower GDP per capita tend to have higher shares of informal employment. In the upper-left quadrant, nations such as the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Chad, Tanzania, and Somalia cluster with informal employment shares between 80% and nearly 100%, alongside GDP per capita figures generally below $3,000. India and Pakistan also sit in this high-informality range, with shares exceeding 80%. Moving toward the center, countries like Indonesia, Egypt, and Thailand show mid-range informal employment levels between 60% and 80%, with GDP per capita values roughly between $5,000 and $15,000. In the lower-right section, countries including South Korea, Spain, and Germany exhibit low informal employment shares, mostly below 20%, and high GDP per capita figures exceeding $30,000. Regional color coding indicates that African nations (purple) predominantly occupy the high-informality, low-GDP area, while European (blue) and North American (orange) countries cluster in the low-informality, high-GDP area. The note specifies that employment definitions align with the 13th ICLS, including subsistence farming, and GDP figures are adjusted to 2021 prices.
